---
title: "Cluing & Fairness"
aliases: ["Fair Play", "Clue Construction", "Solvability"]
family: "Puzzles, Clues & Information"
media: [interactive-fiction, live-game, tabletop, puzzle-hunt]
facets:
  effects: [earned-discovery, solver-trust, forward-momentum]
  problems: [unsolvable-puzzle, dead-end, unearned-reveal]
  components: [clue-text, evidence, answer-key, convention-set]
  genre: [mystery, detective]
provenance: "researched"
---

*Silhouette: The contract that says a solver who pays attention can get there from here.*

Cluing is the craft of handing over exactly enough to reach the answer, and no more. A puzzle with no clues is a locked box with no keyhole. A puzzle with the wrong clues is a box with the answer painted on the lid. The work sits between those two failures: each clue earns its place, the whole set points at one answer, and the solver does the reaching.

Fairness is the promise underneath. It says that an attentive person, working only with what the puzzle gave them, can get to the solution without guessing and without secret knowledge. Break that promise and the solver stops trusting you. Once trust is gone they stop reasoning and start flailing, and a puzzle they cannot trust is one they cannot enjoy.

This article is about the promise and how to keep it. The same promise turns up under different names across the media: "fair play" in a detective story, the "Three Clue Rule" at a tabletop, "no tricks" in a puzzle hunt. They are one idea wearing four coats.

## The Mechanism (How it works)

### What makes a puzzle fair

A puzzle is fair when a sufficiently attentive solver can reach the right answer using only what the puzzle provides, plus conventions that were established somewhere they could find them: inside the puzzle, across the event, or in the wider solving community. [corpus] The test is reachability, not ease. A fair puzzle can still be hard, and a hard puzzle is not unfair just because it is hard. Fairness is a property of the clues themselves: whether each one is sound and whether the set makes the answer reachable. That is a different question from the order the clues arrive in, which is [Clue Sequencing & Chains](clue-sequencing-and-chains.qmd). A puzzle can be perfectly fair yet badly sequenced, or elegantly sequenced yet unfair, so fix fairness first.

It helps to name the ways fairness breaks, because each one has a fix. The list below is drawn from puzzle-hunt practice, but every line has a cousin in the detective story and the tabletop mystery.

| Failure | What it looks like | Why it is unfair |
|---|---|---|
| Underdetermination | The clues fit several answers and nothing settles which | The solver cannot confirm they are right [corpus] |
| Obscure-without-recourse | A fact is needed but there is no signposted way to look it up | The path is closed, not just hidden [corpus] |
| Arbitrary selection | "Take the third item" when nothing marks the third | The choice is unmotivated [corpus] |
| Inconsistency | The same kind of clue behaves differently in the same puzzle, unsignalled | The solver cannot rely on what they learned [corpus] |

Notice what is not on the list: misdirection. Leading a solver's attention to the wrong thing is fair, as long as the wrong thing has an exit and the exit is reachable. [corpus] In a detective story this is the red herring, and the rule is the same: a red herring is not a lie, it is a true thing that points the wrong way and is eventually explained. [researched] Christie's technique was to keep the dramatic focus on the herring while the real clue sat in plain view. [corpus]

### Every clue does work

The second principle is that nothing on the page is decorative by accident. Anything a solver might read as a clue (the flavour text, the order of a list, an odd capital letter, the title) should either do real work or be deliberately neutral. [corpus] A clue that does nothing is worse than no clue, because it sends the solver down a path that goes nowhere.

This forces a choice about conventions, and the choice has to hold. If unusual capitals signal a hidden message in one puzzle, solvers will hunt for hidden messages every time they see unusual capitals. [corpus] Flavour text is the classic case. In some hunts it is pure scene-setting and never clues anything. In others every word of it counts. Both are legitimate. Mixing them inside one event quietly destroys solver trust, because the solver can no longer tell when to read closely. [corpus] The 2014 MIT Mystery Hunt resolved this by ruling that flavour text was never required to solve a puzzle: an explicit commitment, declared up front, so solvers knew where they stood. [corpus]

### The cluing gradient

Clues do not all point with the same directness, and the craft lives in choosing how indirect to be. A direct clue ("the answer is a colour") barely qualifies as a puzzle; it is really a hint. An indirect clue makes the solver identify the category first: a set of items that share a hidden colour, with no label telling them to look for one. A doubled-indirect clue hides the category itself, so the solver must first work out *what kind of thing* they are looking for before they can look for it. [corpus]

Most puzzle hunts live at the indirect and doubled-indirect level. The line that matters is between indirection and obscurity. Indirection means the solver has to work, but every step, once found, is clearly a step. Obscurity means the path is not findable even with full attention. [corpus] The discipline is to make each move hard to *make* but easy to *recognise* once made. How indirect you can safely be is set by the attention you can reasonably expect from a solver, which is the other half of this craft: [Search, Observation & Lateral Thinking](search-observation-and-lateral-thinking.qmd).

### Clue versus gate

A clue enables progress. A gate blocks it until a condition is met. [corpus] "The initials spell a word" is a clue: the solver can find it from the materials in front of them. "Enter the previous answer to continue" is a gate: it needs something from outside this puzzle.

Gates are fine. Puzzle hunts run on them, and so do escape rooms, where finding the key opens the lock that reveals the code. The rule is that a gate must look like a gate. A puzzle that seems solvable from its own materials but secretly needs another puzzle's answer is not clever, it is misleading. [corpus] In an escape room the same principle says each gate's condition should be legible: solvers should know what they need before they can get it, so they can chase it on purpose rather than trip over it. [corpus] In interactive fiction and escape-room software the clue-versus-gate line is drawn in code rather than on paper, which is its own craft: [Digital Gating & State](../systems-mechanics/digital-gating-and-state.qmd).

### Redundancy: the Three Clue Rule

The puzzle-hunt principles above assume one solver chewing on one puzzle. Move to a tabletop investigation, where players roam freely and can simply walk past your evidence, and fairness needs a second mechanism: redundancy.

The Alexandrian's Three Clue Rule states it plainly. For any conclusion you want the players to reach, include at least three clues pointing at it. [corpus] The reasoning is hard-won and slightly cynical: the players will probably miss the first, ignore the second, and misread the third before making the leap you wanted all along. [corpus] One clue is a single point of failure. Three is a fair chance.

The same shape covers a chokepoint, where players need not a deduction but a way through: for any chokepoint, prepare at least three ways past it. [corpus] The prep cost is real. A mystery that turns on one deduction needs three independent trails of evidence, not one, and you have to budget the preparation time for all three. [corpus] This is why node-based scenario design treats a scenario as a web of information nodes rather than a flowchart (the scenario-wide map of which clues feed which conclusions is [Information Architecture](information-architecture.qmd)): every arrow on a flowchart is a chokepoint, and if the players do not follow it the game stalls. Redundant paths between nodes remove the single points of failure. [corpus]

Interactive fiction inherits the same problem in digital form. Because the player chooses where to go, a crucial clue placed on one branch can be missed entirely. The fix is the same redundancy, plus the option to gate forward progress until the key evidence is in hand, so no branch can strand the player short of a solvable position. [corpus]

### Fair play in the detective tradition

The oldest written version of this contract is the detective story's "fair play" rule, and it is worth knowing because the puzzle forms quietly inherited it. The cardinal rule is that every clue needed to reach the solution is present in the text, findable by an attentive reader, and not dependent on anything the reader could not have known. [corpus]

The Golden Age writers codified this into near-legal form. S.S. Van Dine's "Twenty Rules for Writing Detective Stories" (1928) opens by declaring the detective story a game in which the author must play fair, and insists the reader have equal opportunity with the detective to solve it. [researched] Ronald Knox's "Decalogue" (1929) added its own clauses: the criminal must be named early, the detective must declare every clue he finds, and no unaccountable intuition may hand him the answer. [researched] When the Detection Club formed in 1930, its members swore an oath, written by Dorothy L. Sayers, that their detectives would solve crimes "using those wits which it may please you to bestow upon them" and not by "Divine Revelation, Feminine Intuition, Mumbo Jumbo, Jiggery-Pokery, Coincidence, or Act of God." [researched] Strip the period humour and that is exactly the puzzle-hunt's "no tricks": no answer may depend on a rule the solver was never given.

::: {.callout-note}
### The Specimen
The MIT Mystery Hunt has spent decades building a shared cluing grammar that solvers carry between puzzles: answers arrive as all-caps phrases so that meta-puzzles can extract from them reliably, the structure of a grid is itself data (exactly 26 entries probably means the alphabet), and a puzzle's title often carries the only meta-clue it offers, seeded there as both theme and last-resort hint. [corpus] Evan Chen's puzzle-hunt guides document these conventions for incoming solvers, which makes them double as a designer's checklist of what solvers will already expect. [corpus]
:::

## Design Judgement

::: {.callout-tip}
### The Field Note
When a clue is not landing, resist the urge to add a new clue. Ask first whether the clue you have is doing its work and whether the solver can tell it is a clue at all. A cleaner signal usually beats a louder one. Then, separately, count your redundancy: if a single conclusion rests on a single clue, you have a single point of failure, whatever medium you are in. In a hunt that means a fresh-eyes test-solve; at a tabletop it means a second and third trail; in interactive fiction it means the clue appears on more than one branch. The fix for "they got stuck" is almost never one more arrow on the flowchart. [synthesis]
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
### The Hazard
The seductive failure is the clever rule only you know. You invent an elegant step, fall in love with it, and forget the solver was never told it exists. From the inside it reads as inevitable; from the outside it is a wall with no handholds. The tell is when your own playtest notes say "they should have realised" more than once. "Should have realised" is the designer's voice covering an unfair gap. The solver does not owe you the leap. If the path was not reachable from the materials, the puzzle was not fair, however beautiful the intended solution. [synthesis]
:::

## Where this fits

**The bigger picture.** Fairness is the floor the rest of this family stands on. Every other puzzle concern assumes it: that the clues are sound and the answer is reachable. It is the one property you cannot trade away for cleverness, because the moment a solver stops trusting the puzzle they stop reasoning, and a puzzle nobody reasons about has no craft left to admire. How the clues are ordered, how they are themed, how the payoff lands: all of it is built on top of this contract. Get the contract wrong and none of the rest can save the puzzle; get it right and all of it becomes possible.

**Next steps.** Once a puzzle is fair, the craft turns to the payoff that fair cluing has earned: [The Aha Moment](the-aha-moment.qmd). When fair clues still leave solvers stuck for reasons no extra clue will fix, the graceful exit is [Hint Systems & Recovery](hint-systems-and-recovery.qmd). To learn which fairness pitfalls a given kind of puzzle is most prone to, so you know what to check before you build, start from [A Taxonomy of Puzzles](a-taxonomy-of-puzzles.qmd). And in tabletop or live play, where redundant clue trails have to keep paying off as the world shifts around them, those trails are tracked against [Progress Clocks & Faction State](../systems-mechanics/progress-clocks-and-faction-state.qmd).

## Sources

- Wilson, D. "Introduction to Writing Good Puzzle Hunt Puzzles." MIT. <http://web.mit.edu/dwilson/www/puzzles/puzzlewriting.html>
- "Thinking About A Puzzle." MIT Mystery Hunt / Puzzle Club. <https://puzzles.mit.edu/resources/thinkingaboutpuzzles.html>
- Chen, Evan. "Puzzlehunting" guides. <https://web.evanchen.cc/puzzlehunt.html>
- Selinker, Mike and Snyder, Thomas. *Puzzlecraft*. Lone Shark Games, 2012. Chapters on clue construction.
- Alexander, Justin. "The Three Clue Rule." The Alexandrian, 8 May 2008. <https://thealexandrian.net/wordpress/1118/roleplaying-games/three-clue-rule>
- Alexander, Justin. "Node-Based Scenario Design, Part 1." The Alexandrian, 27 May 2010. <https://thealexandrian.net/wordpress/7949/roleplaying-games/node-based-scenario-design-part-1-the-plotted-approach>
- Van Dine, S.S. "Twenty Rules for Writing Detective Stories." *The American Magazine*, September 1928. <http://gaslight-lit.s3-website.ca-central-1.amazonaws.com/gaslight/vandine.htm>
- Knox, Ronald. "The Detective Story Decalogue," introduction to *The Best English Detective Stories of 1928* (1929). Wikipedia summary: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ronald_Knox>
- "Detection Club" (Sayers's initiation oath and fair-play conventions). Wikipedia. <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Detection_Club>
